title: Inspection Materials: Wet and Dry Particles
narrationScript: Every magnetic particle test is really two systems working together. The magnetizing equipment builds a field in the part, and the particles turn an invisible leakage field into something your eye can find. This lesson is about that second system, the particles themselves, and about the application technique that makes or breaks the test. Good particles share four traits. They are finely divided ferromagnetic material with very high permeability, so even a weak leakage field can capture them. They have low retentivity, so they do not stay magnetized, clump together, and stick where there is no flaw. They come in a controlled blend of sizes and shapes, fine enough to respond to small leakage fields, varied enough to flow well and bridge a crack. And they contrast strongly with the surface, either by color under white light or by glowing green yellow under ultraviolet light. Dry particles are a free flowing powder applied from a squeeze bulb, a shaker, or a powder gun. The blend matters. Rounded grains flow and tumble across the surface, while elongated grains build a visible line once a leakage field catches them. Dry powder is the field favorite for rough surfaces, as welded and as cast work, because the particles tumble over surface texture that would trap a wet bath. Many powders can be used on hot parts, with some rated to around six hundred degrees Fahrenheit, which makes dry method practical on weldments that are still warm. Paired with half wave rectified current and prods or a yoke, dry powder also gives the best practical shot at near surface flaws, because the pulsating current keeps the particles dancing and walking toward weak, broad leakage fields. The tradeoffs are real. Dry powder is less sensitive to very fine, tight surface cracks than a wet bath, wind can blow the cloud away outdoors, moisture ruins its flow, and once it hits the floor it is done. You do not reclaim and reuse dry powder. Wet particles are much finer, and they ride in a liquid carrier, either conditioned water or a low viscosity oil. Because the particles are smaller, they respond to the very weak leakage fields of fine, shallow flaws, things like grinding cracks and early fatigue cracks that dry powder can miss. The bath flows over the whole surface, so coverage is complete and even, which suits production work on machined parts. On a bench unit the bath recirculates, so concentration has to be controlled. The standard tool is a settling test in a graduated centrifuge tube. Typical procedure values run about one point two to two point four milliliters of settled visible particles per one hundred milliliters of bath, and about zero point one to zero point four milliliters for fluorescent particles. Wet method has temperature limits far below dry powder, because the carrier evaporates or flashes, so hot work stays with dry. Now the part that separates trained hands from powder dumpers, application. The standard technique is the continuous method, which means the magnetizing current is flowing while the particles are applied. The leakage field at a flaw is strongest while the current is on, and for most parts there is little field left after it stops. With dry powder, float a light cloud of particles onto the surface while the shot is on. Let them drift and settle. Never throw or pour powder at the part. Then, still under current, remove the excess with a gentle stream of air, just strong enough to lift loose powder but not strong enough to strip particles held by a leakage field. Watch the surface the whole time, because indications form as you dust. With the wet method the order changes. Flood the surface gently until it is fully wetted, divert the hose away, and then fire the shot, typically two or more pulses of around half a second, while the bath drains off the part. If you keep flooding during or after the shot, the moving liquid washes developing indications right off the surface. One more habit belongs in this lesson. A leakage field forms best when flux crosses a discontinuity at forty five to ninety degrees. One shot in one direction is half an inspection. Plan your particle applications around two roughly perpendicular magnetizations, and apply particles for each one. Particles are cheap. Missed cracks are not. Apply them like the precision detection medium they are, and the method will show you everything it can.
imagePrompt: A technician in safety glasses dusts a light cloud of gray dry magnetic powder from a squeeze bulb onto a rusty welded steel plate clamped under an energized magnetic yoke in a fabrication shop.

sections.heading: The Particle Is the Detector
sections.body:
The magnetizing equipment only builds the field — the **particles** are the detector. A discontinuity that crosses flux lines forces a small leakage field out of the surface, and the particles bridge that leakage field and hold there, turning an invisible flaw into a visible line.

Good inspection particles share four engineered properties:

- **High permeability** — they respond strongly even to the weak leakage field of a fine flaw.
- **Low retentivity** — they do not stay magnetized, so they neither clump together nor cling to clean surface.
- **Controlled size and shape blend** — fine enough for sensitivity, with rounded grains for mobility and elongated grains that align and build a visible indication.
- **Contrast** — strong color difference under white light, or green-yellow fluorescence under UV-A.

Two families exist: **dry powder** applied as a cloud, and **wet particles** suspended in a liquid bath. Choosing between them — and applying them correctly — is the substance of this lesson.

sections.heading: Dry Particles
sections.body:
Dry particles are a free-flowing powder applied from a squeeze bulb, mechanical shaker, or powder gun. They come in visible colors — gray, red, black, yellow — selected for contrast against the test surface.

Where dry powder earns its keep:

- **Rough surfaces.** As-welded and as-cast textures trap a wet bath, but dry grains tumble across them and still collect at leakage fields.
- **Hot work.** Liquid carriers evaporate or flash, but many dry powders are usable on hot parts, some rated to roughly **600 °F** — check the manufacturer's rating and your procedure.
- **Near-surface flaws.** Paired with **half-wave rectified current (HWDC)**, the pulsating field keeps grains mobile and "walking" toward the broad, weak leakage fields of slightly subsurface discontinuities. This combination is the classic structural-weld field technique.

Limitations: dry powder is **less sensitive to very fine, tight surface cracks** than a wet bath; wind disperses the cloud outdoors; moisture and oil contamination destroy its flow; and it is single-use — powder is never swept up and reused, because contaminated, work-hardened powder loses both mobility and permeability.

sections.caption: Dry-powder technique: a light cloud applied while the current is on, with excess removed by gentle air so leakage fields keep their indications.

sections.heading: Wet Particles
sections.body:
Wet-method particles are **far finer** than dry grains and are suspended in a liquid carrier — **conditioned water** (with wetting agent, corrosion inhibitor, and antifoam additives) or a **low-viscosity oil**. Because the particles are smaller, they respond to the very weak leakage fields of fine, shallow discontinuities: grinding cracks, heat-treat cracks, and early fatigue cracks that dry powder can miss outright.

The bath flows over the entire surface, giving complete, even coverage on machined production parts, and on a bench unit it **recirculates** — which is why concentration control matters. Concentration is checked with a **settling test** in a graduated centrifuge tube; typical procedure values are about **1.2–2.4 mL settled solids per 100 mL** of bath for visible particles and **0.1–0.4 mL per 100 mL** for fluorescent. Too lean and indications come up faint; too rich and background masks them.

Wet particles come visible (black or red) and **fluorescent** — the dominant choice on bench units, where a darkened booth and UV-A light make a fine crack blaze green-yellow. Temperature limits are low compared to dry powder, so hot parts stay with the dry method.

sections.caption: Wet-method sequence on a bench unit: flood the part, divert the bath, then energize the shot while the suspension drains.
Dry powder versus wet suspension — typical selection logic.
rows: Much finer — highest sensitivity to fine surface cracks
Excellent on rough, as-welded, as-cast surfaces
Best on smooth or machined surfaces
Usable hot; some powders rated to ≈ 600 °F
Low limits — carrier evaporates or flashes
Strong with HWDC and prods or yoke
Possible, but dry + HWDC is the field favorite
Bench units, production and overhaul inspection
Recirculated bath — concentration checked by settling test

sections.title: Continuous method: current ON while particles go on
sections.body: The leakage field at a flaw is strongest **while the magnetizing current flows**, and on low-retentivity parts almost nothing remains afterward. So the standard technique is the **continuous method**: dry powder is dusted on and the excess blown off **while the shot is on**; a wet bath is flooded on, the flow diverted, and **then** the shot fired as the bath drains. The **residual method** — applying particles after magnetization — is reserved for high-retentivity materials and only when your written procedure allows it.

sections.heading: Application Technique
sections.body:
**Dry powder:** float a *light cloud* of particles onto the energized surface and let it drift down — never throw, pour, or pile powder on the part. A buried surface shows nothing. While the current is still on, remove excess with a **gentle stream of air**, strong enough to lift loose grains but not strong enough to strip particles held by a leakage field. Watch the surface continuously; indications form as you dust, and a forming indication tells you where to look harder.

**Wet method:** flood the surface gently until fully wetted, **divert the hose**, then energize — typically two or more shots of about a half second while the bath drains. Flooding during or after the shot washes developing indications away. For portable wet work, aerosol cans of premixed suspension follow the same logic: spray, stop, shoot.

**Both methods:** detectability is best when flux crosses the discontinuity at **45–90°**, so plan two roughly perpendicular magnetizations and apply particles for each. Coverage means every area sees adequate field in two directions — with particles applied correctly both times.

figures.title: Dry-powder application with the continuous method
Wet-method application sequence on a bench unit

checkQuestions.question: During a dry-powder examination with a yoke, when should the magnetizing current be flowing?
options: Only while powder is applied — switch off before blowing away the excess
While powder is applied and while the excess is removed with gentle air
Only after all powder is applied and the excess removed
Current timing makes no difference with dry powder
checkQuestions.explanation: In the continuous method the leakage field holds the indication only while current flows, so both application and excess removal happen under power — switching off first lets the gentle air strip real indications.

checkQuestions.question: A structural weld is still at roughly 450 °F and has an as-welded surface. The appropriate particle choice is:
options: Fluorescent wet bath in a water carrier
Visible wet bath in an oil carrier
Dry powder rated for elevated temperature
Any particle — temperature does not affect the medium
checkQuestions.explanation: Liquid carriers evaporate or flash on hot work, while many dry powders are rated to around 600 °F and tumble well over rough as-welded surfaces.

checkQuestions.question: Which medium gives the best chance of finding very fine grinding cracks on a smooth machined journal, and why?
options: Dry powder — coarse grains bridge fine cracks best
Wet fluorescent particles — finer particles respond to the weak leakage fields of fine flaws
Dry powder — it is more sensitive on smooth surfaces
Either medium — sensitivity is identical on machined parts
checkQuestions.explanation: Wet-method particles are far finer than dry grains, so they are captured by the very weak leakage fields of fine shallow cracks — and fluorescence under UV-A maximizes contrast.

checkQuestions.question: The correct way to apply dry powder is to:
options: Pour a generous layer so no area is missed, then brush it off
Float a light cloud onto the energized part and remove excess with gentle air
Throw powder at the surface so it impacts and sticks at flaws
Apply powder before energizing so it is in place when the field appears
checkQuestions.explanation: A light drifting cloud lets leakage fields capture particles as they settle; piles bury indications, and application before energizing defeats the continuous method on low-retentivity parts.

checkQuestions.question: In the wet continuous method, the magnetizing shot is fired:
options: While the bath is flooding the surface, for maximum particle supply
After the bath flow is diverted, while the suspension drains over the part
Before any bath is applied, to pre-magnetize the surface
Several minutes after the bath has fully dried
checkQuestions.explanation: Flood, divert, then shoot: a flowing bath during or after the shot washes developing indications off the part, while draining suspension leaves mobile particles available as the field peaks.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §7.3 (application of particles)
Practices such as ASTM E709 and ASTM E1444 (particle media, bath concentration, application — general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (method requirements — general practice)
